verses.flow: Now these are the names of the sons of Israel who came to Egypt with Jacob; each one came with his household:
verses.note: The Hebrew title of Exodus is 'shemot' -- "Names." The book opens by listing the names of Jacob's sons, connecting directly to Genesis 46:8-27. In the biblical worldview, names carry identity and destiny; God knows His people by name (Isa 43:1). The opening word 've'elleh' ("and these") with the conjunction "and" signals that Exodus is not a new book but a continuation of an unfinished story.

verses.flow: And all the persons who came from the loins of Jacob were seventy souls, and Joseph was already in Egypt.
verses.note: The number seventy is significant throughout Scripture: seventy nations in the Table of Nations (Gen 10), seventy elders of Israel (Exod 24:1), seventy years of exile (Jer 25:11), seventy weeks of Daniel (Dan 9:24). The LXX reads seventy-five, which Stephen follows in Acts 7:14, likely counting additional descendants through Joseph's line.

verses.flow: But the sons of Israel were fruitful and swarmed and multiplied and became exceedingly mighty, and the land was filled with them.
verses.note: The Hebrew verbs here -- 'paru' (were fruitful), 'vayyishretsu' (swarmed), 'vayyirbu' (multiplied) -- deliberately echo Genesis 1:28, the creation mandate. Israel's growth in Egypt is presented as a fulfillment of both the creation blessing and the Abrahamic promise (Gen 15:5, 22:17). Pharaoh's attempt to suppress them is thus a war against God's creational purposes.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Now a new king arose over Egypt who did not know Joseph.
verses.note: The phrase "who did not know Joseph" ('asher lo yada et Yosef') is more than historical amnesia. The Hebrew 'yada' (to know) implies relational acknowledgment and covenant loyalty. This new king refused to honor the covenant relationship Joseph had established. Most scholars identify this Pharaoh with either Ahmose I (who expelled the Hyksos and may have viewed Semites with suspicion) or a later 18th/19th Dynasty ruler.

verses.flow: So they set taskmasters over them to afflict them with their burdens. And they built storage cities for Pharaoh: Pithom and Raamses.
verses.note: The store cities 'Pithom' (Egyptian: Per-Atum, "House of Atum") and 'Raamses' (Pi-Ramesses) are archaeologically attested in the eastern Nile Delta. The mention of Raamses is the primary evidence for the late date of the exodus (~1260 BC under Ramesses II), though conservative scholars argue the name is an editorial update of an older place name (cf. Gen 47:11, which uses "Rameses" for a region in Joseph's time, centuries before any Pharaoh Ramesses).

verses.flow: Then the king of Egypt spoke to the Hebrew midwives, of whom the name of one was Shiphrah and the name of the other was Puah,
verses.note: The Hebrew midwives 'Shiphrah' and 'Puah' are among the first named women to practice civil disobedience in Scripture. The phrase "Hebrew midwives" ('hameyalledot ha'ivriyyot') is ambiguous in Hebrew -- it can mean "midwives who were Hebrews" or "midwives to the Hebrews" (i.e., Egyptian women serving Hebrew clients). Most interpreters take them as Hebrew women. Remarkably, the names of these two lowly women are preserved while the mighty Pharaoh remains unnamed -- a deliberate irony.

verses.flow: But the midwives feared God, and did not do as the king of Egypt had commanded them, but let the boys live.
verses.note: The midwives "feared God" ('vattirenah ha'meyalledot et ha'Elohim') -- the same phrase used of Abraham (Gen 22:12) and later of those who will receive wisdom (Prov 1:7, 9:10). Fear of God overrode fear of Pharaoh. This is the first recorded instance of believers defying an unjust government on the grounds of a higher authority -- a principle echoed in Acts 5:29: "We must obey God rather than men." God rewarded their faithfulness not despite their deception of Pharaoh, but because of their allegiance to Him.

verses.flow: And it came about because the midwives feared God, that He established households for them.
verses.note: God "established households" ('vayyaas lahem battim') for the midwives. The Hebrew 'battim' (houses/households) implies dynastic families -- God gave them descendants and lasting legacy. This is the consistent biblical pattern: those who risk everything for God's purposes receive more than they sacrificed (cf. Mark 10:29-30). Their obedience preserved the line from which Moses -- and ultimately Christ -- would come.

verses.flow: Then Pharaoh commanded all his people, saying, "Every son who is born you shall cast into the Nile, but every daughter you shall let live."
verses.note: Pharaoh's decree to throw Hebrew sons into the Nile is the first of several "slaughter of innocents" events in Scripture that foreshadow Herod's massacre in Bethlehem (Matt 2:16-18). In both cases, a paranoid ruler tries to destroy a deliverer by killing infant boys; in both cases, God preserves the chosen child. The Nile -- Egypt's source of life and the dwelling place of the god Hapi -- becomes an instrument of death.

verses.flow: But when she could hide him no longer, she took for him an ark of bulrushes and covered it with tar and with pitch, and she placed the child in it and set it among the reeds at the bank of the Nile.
verses.note: The word for "ark" here is 'tevah' -- the exact same word used for Noah's ark in Genesis 6-9, and it appears ONLY in these two passages in the entire Hebrew Bible. This is a deliberate literary connection: just as Noah was saved through water in a 'tevah' to deliver humanity, Moses is saved through water in a 'tevah' to deliver Israel. Both are vessels of salvation coated with pitch ('kopher' in Gen 6:14, 'chemar va'zepheth' here). The typology extends to Christ: salvation comes through water (baptism, 1 Pet 3:20-21) and through One who passed through death to bring deliverance.

verses.flow: And the child grew, and she brought him to Pharaoh's daughter, and he became her son. And she named him Moses, and said, "Because I drew him out of the water."
verses.note: The name 'Mosheh' (Moses) is given an Israelite etymology: "I drew him out" ('meshitihu') from the water. However, the name is also recognizably Egyptian -- 'mose/mes' means "born of" or "child" in Egyptian (as in Thutmose = "born of Thoth," Ramesses = "born of Ra"). This dual etymology is significant: Moses belongs to both worlds. He is a Hebrew child with an Egyptian name, raised in Pharaoh's court but destined to lead Israel out. He is also a type of Christ, who was "drawn out" from death and who bridges heaven and earth.

verses.flow: Now it came about in those days, when Moses had grown up, that he went out to his brothers and looked on their burdens; and he saw an Egyptian striking a Hebrew, one of his brothers.
verses.note: Moses was forty years old when he went out to his brothers (Acts 7:23). His life divides into three forties: 40 years in Egypt's palace, 40 years in Midian's wilderness, 40 years leading Israel. This pattern of preparation through obscurity is repeated throughout Scripture -- Joseph in prison, David in the wilderness, Paul in Arabia, Jesus' 40 days of testing.

verses.flow: Now when Pharaoh heard of this matter, he sought to kill Moses. But Moses fled from the presence of Pharaoh and settled in the land of Midian, and he sat down by a well.
verses.note: Moses' flight to Midian parallels Jacob's flight to Haran (Gen 28-29): both flee a death threat, both arrive at a well, both help women at the well, both marry into the family. The "well scene" is a biblical type-scene (cf. Gen 24, John 4). Midian was descended from Abraham through Keturah (Gen 25:2), so Moses married within the broader Abrahamic family.

verses.flow: And God heard their groaning; and God remembered His covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob.
verses.note: Four verbs describe God's response: He "heard" ('shama'), "remembered" ('zakar'), "saw" ('ra'ah'), and "knew/took notice" ('yada'). God's "remembering" does not imply He had forgotten -- 'zakar' means to act upon a prior commitment. When God "remembers" His covenant, He activates it. This is the theological engine of the entire exodus: God acts because of His covenant with Abraham (Gen 15:13-14), not because Israel earned deliverance.

verses.flow: Now Moses was pasturing the flock of Jethro his father-in-law, the priest of Midian; and he led the flock to the west side of the wilderness and came to Horeb, the mountain of God.
verses.note: Moses leads the flock to "Horeb, the mountain of God." 'Horeb' and 'Sinai' appear to be two names for the same location (or the same mountain range). The name 'Chorev' may derive from 'charev' (dry/desolate). That God reveals Himself on a barren mountain in the wilderness -- not in a palace or temple -- is theologically significant: God is not found in human grandeur but meets people in desolate places of preparation.

verses.flow: And the angel of the LORD appeared to him in a flame of fire from the midst of a bush; and he looked, and behold, the bush was burning with fire, yet the bush was not consumed.
verses.note: The "angel of the LORD" ('malakh YHWH') appears -- then the text seamlessly transitions to God Himself speaking (v.4: "God called to him from the midst of the bush"). This conflation of the angel of the LORD with YHWH Himself occurs repeatedly in the OT (Gen 16:7-13, 22:11-18, Judg 6:11-23, 13:3-22) and is a major piece of evidence for the pre-incarnate Christ in the OT. The angel is simultaneously distinct from YHWH and yet identified as YHWH -- a pattern that anticipates the Trinity.

verses.note: Removing sandals signifies that the ground is holy -- set apart by God's presence. The only other place this command appears is Joshua 5:15, where the commander of the LORD's army tells Joshua the same thing. In both cases, it marks the beginning of a divine commission for deliverance. The ground is not holy in itself; it becomes holy because God is present there.

verses.flow: So I have come down to deliver them from the hand of the Egyptians, and to bring them up from that land to a good and spacious land, to a land flowing with milk and honey, to the place of the Canaanite, the Hittite, the Amorite, the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite.
verses.note: The phrase "a land flowing with milk and honey" ('eretz zavat chalav u'devash') appears over twenty times in the OT. Milk represents pastoral abundance (flocks), and honey represents agricultural richness (either bee honey or date syrup, 'devash' can mean either). This is the land promised to Abraham (Gen 15:18-21), now being activated after 430 years (Gal 3:17). The promised land is also a type of the believer's inheritance in Christ (Heb 4:1-11).

verses.flow: And God said to Moses, "I AM WHO I AM"; and He said, "Thus you shall say to the sons of Israel, 'I AM has sent me to you.'"
verses.note: The divine name 'Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh' -- "I AM WHO I AM" (or "I WILL BE WHAT I WILL BE") -- is the most significant theological statement in the OT. The verb 'hayah' (to be) in the imperfect tense conveys ongoing, uncaused, self-existent being. God is not defined by anything outside Himself. The name YHWH (the Tetragrammaton) is derived from this same root. When Jesus said 'ego eimi' -- "I AM" -- in John 8:58 ("Before Abraham was, I AM"), the Jews immediately picked up stones because they understood He was claiming to be the God of the burning bush. See also John 8:24, 8:28, 13:19, 18:5-6 (where the soldiers fall backward at His "I AM"). Jesus' seven "I AM" statements in John (bread, light, door, shepherd, resurrection, way, vine) are explicit claims to deity rooted in this verse.

verses.flow: And God said further to Moses, "Thus you shall say to the sons of Israel, 'The LORD, the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, has sent me to you.' This is My name forever, and this is My memorial-name to all generations.
verses.note: God declares this name is "My memorial-name to all generations" ('zeh zikri ledor dor'). The name YHWH is not merely a label but a revelation of God's character -- eternal, self-existent, covenant-faithful. Jewish tradition later avoided speaking the name aloud, substituting 'Adonai' (Lord), which is why most English Bibles render YHWH as "the LORD" (small caps). The actual pronunciation was likely 'Yahweh,' preserved in theophoric names ('Yeho-' prefix, '-yahu' suffix) and Greek transliterations by early church fathers (Clement, Theodoret).

verses.note: The request for a "three days' journey" to sacrifice appears deceptive to modern readers, but it functions as a diplomatic test. In ancient Near Eastern convention, this was a modest, reasonable request. Pharaoh's refusal to grant even this small concession reveals the absolute nature of his defiance. God already knows Pharaoh will refuse (v.19) -- the request exposes Pharaoh's heart, not God's intentions.

verses.note: God's anger at Moses' final objection ("Please send someone else") results in Aaron being appointed as spokesman. This has far-reaching consequences: Aaron's role eventually leads to the golden calf incident (ch. 32) and a priesthood that becomes the pattern for Israel's worship. Moses' reluctance shaped Israel's future in ways he could not have foreseen -- a sober reminder that our resistance to God's call has consequences beyond ourselves.

verses.flow: Then you shall say to Pharaoh, 'Thus says the LORD: Israel is My son, My firstborn.
verses.note: "Israel is My firstborn son" ('beni bekhori Yisrael') is one of the most theologically loaded statements in the OT. Firstborn ('bekhor') status means preeminence, inheritance rights, and special relationship with the father. God claims Israel as His firstborn among the nations -- not because Israel was first in time, but first in relationship. This sets up the tenth plague logic: Pharaoh refuses to release God's firstborn, so God takes Pharaoh's firstborn. More profoundly, this title ultimately belongs to Christ -- "the firstborn over all creation" (Col 1:15), "the firstborn from the dead" (Col 1:18), "the firstborn among many brothers" (Rom 8:29). Israel as "firstborn" is typological -- it points forward to the true Firstborn Son.

verses.flow: Now it came about at a lodging place on the way that the LORD met him and sought to put him to death.
verses.note: The enigmatic "bridegroom of blood" incident (vv.24-26) is one of the most debated passages in the OT. God seeks to kill Moses (or his son), and Zipporah circumcises their son, touching the foreskin to Moses' feet (possibly a euphemism). The best reading is that Moses, who will command Israel to keep the Passover (blood on doorposts), has neglected the covenant sign of circumcision in his own household. God cannot send an uncircumcised mediator to call a circumcised people. The blood of circumcision foreshadows the blood of the Passover -- both are covenant signs that avert divine judgment.

verses.flow: And the people believed; and when they heard that the LORD had visited the sons of Israel and that He had seen their affliction, they bowed low and worshiped.
verses.note: The people "bowed low and worshiped" ('vayyiqdu vayyishtachavu') when they heard that God had "visited" ('paqad') the sons of Israel. The verb 'paqad' means to attend to, to take notice with intent to act -- it implies intervention, not mere observation. This is the same verb used in Genesis 50:24-25 when Joseph prophesied: "God will surely visit you" ('paqod yifqod'). Joseph's prophecy, spoken 400 years earlier, is now being fulfilled.

verses.note: Pharaoh's response -- "Who is the LORD ('YHWH'), that I should obey His voice?" -- is the defining question of Exodus. The entire plague narrative exists to answer it. By the end, even Egypt will "know that I am the LORD" (7:5, 14:4, 14:18). The irony is devastating: Pharaoh asks who YHWH is, and YHWH spends ten plagues introducing Himself.

verses.note: Moses' complaint -- "Why have You brought harm to this people?" -- is the first of many honest protests Moses brings before God. Unlike pagan prayer, which demanded appeasement, biblical prayer permits raw honesty. Job, David, Jeremiah, and Habakkuk all bring similar complaints. God does not rebuke Moses for this -- He responds with a deeper revelation of His name and plan (6:1-8).

Things got worse before they got better -- a pattern common in Scripture (the cross before the resurrection, tribulation before glory). verses.flow: and I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, as God Almighty, but by My name, the LORD, I did not make Myself known to them.
verses.note: "I appeared to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob as God Almighty ('El Shaddai'), but by My name the LORD ('YHWH') I did not make Myself known to them." This is one of the most discussed verses in the Pentateuch. It does NOT mean the patriarchs never heard the name YHWH (Genesis uses it freely -- Gen 4:26, 12:8, 15:7). Rather, they did not EXPERIENCE what the name means -- the God who keeps impossible covenant promises across generations. 'El Shaddai' emphasizes power and provision; 'YHWH' emphasizes covenant faithfulness that acts in history. God is now about to demonstrate what His name means through the exodus.

verses.flow: Therefore say to the sons of Israel, 'I am the LORD, and I will bring you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians, and I will deliver you from their bondage. I will also redeem you with an outstretched arm and with great judgments.
verses.note: God's seven "I will" statements (vv.6-8) form the theological backbone of the exodus: I will bring you out, I will deliver you, I will redeem you, I will take you for My people, I will be your God, I will bring you to the land, I will give it to you. These seven promises move from liberation to relationship to inheritance -- the complete arc of salvation. The four cups of the Passover Seder are traditionally linked to the first four of these promises. Jesus took the cup at the Last Supper (Luke 22:20) within this liturgical framework.

These are the heads of their fathers' households. The sons of Reuben, Israel's firstborn: Hanoch and Pallu, Hezron and Carmi; these are the families of Reuben.
And the sons of Simeon: Jemuel and Jamin and Ohad and Jachin and Zohar and Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman; these are the families of Simeon.
And these are the names of the sons of Levi according to their generations: Gershon and Kohath and Merari; and the years of the life of Levi were one hundred and thirty-seven.
verses.note: The genealogy (vv.14-25) seems to interrupt the narrative, but it serves a vital purpose: it establishes Moses and Aaron's precise lineage within Levi, anchoring the priesthood and prophetic authority in a specific family line. This is the biblical equivalent of legal credentials -- proving that the men who confront Pharaoh and establish Israel's worship have legitimate standing.

verses.flow: The sons of Gershon: Libni and Shimei, according to their families.

And the sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. These are the families of the Levites according to their generations.

verses.flow: And Amram married Jochebed his father's sister, and she bore him Aaron and Moses; and the years of the life of Amram were one hundred and thirty-seven.
verses.note: Amram married his father's sister Jochebed -- a marriage later prohibited by Levitical law (Lev 18:12). This is one of several indicators that the Torah's laws were not retroactively applied to the patriarchal period. The law given at Sinai governed Israel from Sinai forward; the patriarchs lived under the Abrahamic covenant, not the Mosaic.

verses.flow: Then the LORD said to Moses, "See, I have made you as God to Pharaoh, and your brother Aaron shall be your prophet.
verses.note: God makes Moses "as God" ('Elohim') to Pharaoh. In the divine council worldview, this means Moses functions as God's authorized representative in the heavenly lawsuit against Egypt. He speaks with divine authority, pronounces divine judgments, and wields divine power. This is the same language used of the 'Elohim' in Psalm 82 who judge the nations.

verses.flow: But I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and I will multiply My signs and My wonders in the land of Egypt.
verses.note: God says He will "harden" ('aqsheh') Pharaoh's heart. Three different Hebrew verbs are used for hardening throughout the plague narrative: 'chazaq' (to strengthen/stiffen, 4:21, 7:13), 'kaved' (to make heavy/dull, 7:14, 8:15), and 'qashah' (to make hard/obstinate, 7:3). In the first five plagues, Pharaoh hardens his own heart; only after repeated self-hardening does God confirm him in his chosen disposition. Romans 9:17-18 cites this as an example of God's sovereign purposes, but the narrative makes clear that Pharaoh's rebellion was his own before God sealed it.

verses.flow: "When Pharaoh speaks to you, saying, 'Work a miracle for yourselves,' then you shall say to Aaron, 'Take your staff and throw it down before Pharaoh, that it may become a serpent.'"
verses.note: Aaron's rod becoming a 'tannin' (serpent/dragon) before Pharaoh is more than a magic trick. 'Tannin' is the word used for the great sea creatures in Genesis 1:21 and for the chaos monster in Isaiah 27:1 and Ezekiel 29:3 -- where Pharaoh himself is called "the great 'tannin' who lies in the midst of his rivers." God turns Moses' rod into the very creature Pharaoh claims to be, and it swallows the Egyptian imitations. The cosmic symbolism is unmistakable.

verses.flow: Then Pharaoh also called for the wise men and the sorcerers, and they also, the magicians of Egypt, did the same with their secret arts.
verses.note: The Egyptian magicians ('chartummim') replicate the sign with their "secret arts" ('lahatim', possibly meaning flames or enchantments). The Egyptian magical tradition was sophisticated and well-documented (the Westcar Papyrus records similar court magic). But note: they can imitate, never reverse. They can make more blood (7:22) and more frogs (8:7), but they cannot remove them. Counterfeit power can mimic God's works but cannot solve the problems they create.

verses.flow: Then the LORD said to Moses, "Pharaoh's heart is stubborn; he refuses to let the people go.
verses.note: The plague cycle begins. The ten plagues follow a structured pattern of three triads plus a climax: (1) Blood, Frogs, Gnats; (2) Flies, Livestock Disease, Boils; (3) Hail, Locusts, Darkness; (10) Death of the Firstborn. Within each triad, the first plague is announced at the Nile in the morning, the second is announced at the palace, and the third comes without warning. This literary structure is deliberate theological composition.

verses.flow: Thus says the LORD: By this you shall know that I am the LORD. Behold, I will strike the water that is in the Nile with the staff that is in my hand, and it will be turned to blood.
verses.note: The Nile turning to blood ('dam') strikes at the heart of Egyptian identity. The Nile was deified as Hapi, the source of all life and prosperity. The annual Nile flood was Egypt's salvation; now it becomes their curse. Exodus 12:12 declares that the plagues are judgments "against all the gods of Egypt." Each plague systematically dismantles a specific Egyptian deity and the worldview built upon it.

verses.flow: But when Pharaoh saw that there was relief, he hardened his heart and did not listen to them, just as the LORD had said.
verses.note: When Pharaoh saw "relief" ('harvachah'), he hardened his heart. The Hebrew 'kaved' (made heavy) describes a deliberate act of dulling himself to what he had just experienced. This is the pattern of sin: the moment pressure lifts, the lesson is forgotten. Pharaoh promised repentance under duress but revoked it the moment the crisis passed -- a pattern Solomon later describes in Ecclesiastes 8:11.

verses.note: The magicians cannot produce gnats ('kinnim') and confess: "This is the finger of God" ('etsba Elohim'). They recognize a power beyond their craft. Jesus uses the same phrase in Luke 11:20: "If I cast out demons by the finger of God, then the kingdom of God has come upon you." The magicians' confession is an unwilling testimony that YHWH's power exceeds all Egyptian magic. From this point, the magicians disappear from the narrative as active participants.

verses.flow: And Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron and said, "Go, sacrifice to your God within the land."
verses.note: Pharaoh offers a compromise -- "sacrifice to your God within the land" (i.e., stay in Egypt). Moses refuses because Egyptian animal worship would make Hebrew sacrifice an "abomination" ('to'avah') to the Egyptians. The bull (Apis), the cow (Hathor), and the ram (Khnum) were sacred in Egypt. This is not merely a logistical problem but a theological one: you cannot worship YHWH on Egypt's terms.

verses.flow: Then the LORD said to Moses, "Rise up early in the morning and stand before Pharaoh and say to him, 'Thus says the LORD, the God of the Hebrews: Let My people go, that they may serve Me.
For this time I will send all My plagues on your heart and on your servants and on your people, so that you may know that there is no one like Me in all the earth.
verses.note: God declares He will send plagues "against your heart" ('el libbeka') -- this is personal, not merely national. The purpose: "so that you may know that there is no one like Me in all the earth." The plagues are revelatory -- they exist to make known who God is. Paul quotes verse 16 in Romans 9:17 as evidence that God raises up even the wicked to display His power and proclaim His name in all the earth.

verses.flow: But indeed, for this very purpose I have raised you up, to show you My power and in order to proclaim My name through all the earth.
verses.note: "For this very purpose I raised you up" ('he'emadtikha') -- God tells Pharaoh that his existence serves a divine purpose. Paul's citation in Romans 9:17 makes this a central text for the doctrine of divine sovereignty. The Hebrew 'he'emad' means "I caused you to stand/remain" -- God sustained Pharaoh through the plagues precisely so the full revelation of divine power could unfold.

verses.flow: Then the LORD said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand toward the sky, that there may be darkness over the land of Egypt, even a darkness which may be felt."
verses.note: The plague of darkness ('choshekh') targets Ra, the supreme deity of the Egyptian pantheon -- the sun god. Pharaoh himself bore the title "Son of Ra." Three days of impenetrable darkness ('choshekh afeilah' -- darkness of thick gloom) is an attack on the very identity of the Egyptian state. The darkness is "felt" ('yamesh') -- tangible, oppressive, supernatural. This recalls the primordial darkness of Genesis 1:2 before God spoke light into existence. God can undo creation itself.

verses.flow: So Moses stretched out his hand toward the sky, and there was thick darkness in all the land of Egypt for three days.
verses.note: "But all the sons of Israel had light in their dwellings." The separation between darkness and light is a creation motif (Gen 1:4) and a consistent biblical metaphor for judgment vs. salvation, death vs. life. John's Gospel is built on this framework: "The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it" (John 1:5). God's people dwell in light even when the world is in darkness.

verses.flow: And Moses said, "Thus says the LORD: 'About midnight I am going out into the midst of Egypt,
verses.note: God Himself will "go out" ('yotse') into the midst of Egypt at "about midnight" ('kachatsot hallailah'). The language is of a king going to war. The plague comes not through a natural agent but through direct divine action. Midnight -- the darkest hour -- is when deliverance comes. This pattern appears repeatedly: the resurrection is discovered at dawn after the darkest night, Paul and Silas are freed at midnight (Acts 16:25).

verses.note: The death falls on every firstborn from "the firstborn of Pharaoh who sits on his throne to the firstborn of the slave girl who is behind the millstones." The social hierarchy is irrelevant -- judgment spans from highest to lowest. The inclusion of the slave girl's son shows that this is not merely political judgment against the ruling class but cosmic judgment against Egypt's gods (12:12).

"This month shall be the beginning of months for you; it is to be the first month of the year to you.
verses.note: "This month shall be the beginning of months for you; it shall be the first month of the year to you." God resets Israel's calendar -- deliverance marks the beginning of time for His people. The month is 'Aviv' (later called 'Nisan'), the spring month. Israel's year begins not with creation but with redemption. Similarly, the Christian reckons time from the cross and resurrection, which occurred during Passover/Nisan.

verses.flow: Speak to all the congregation of Israel, saying, 'On the tenth of this month they are each one to take a lamb for themselves, according to their fathers' households, a lamb for each household.
verses.note: The lamb is selected on the 10th of the month and kept until the 14th -- four days of inspection to ensure it is without blemish. Jesus entered Jerusalem on the 10th of Nisan (Palm Sunday) and was examined by priests, Pharisees, Sadducees, Herodians, and Pilate for four days before being crucified on the 14th. He was found "without blemish" by every examiner: "I find no fault in this man" (Luke 23:4, John 18:38, 19:4, 19:6).

verses.note: The lamb must be "unblemished" ('tamim') -- the same word used for Noah (Gen 6:9, "blameless") and required of all sacrificial animals (Lev 1:3, 22:21). Peter explicitly applies this to Christ: "a lamb without blemish or defect" (1 Pet 1:19). The Passover lamb is perhaps the clearest type of Christ in the entire OT.

verses.note: The blood is applied to the doorposts and lintel -- the top and two sides of the door frame. If you trace the application pattern (top, left side, right side, with the threshold left untouched -- the blood is not to be trampled), it forms the outline of a cross. Hebrews 10:29 warns against "trampling underfoot the Son of God" and regarding "the blood of the covenant as unclean." The blood marks the household as belonging to God; it is the visible sign of faith in His promise.

verses.note: "Against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments -- I am the LORD." This is the interpretive key to the entire plague narrative. The plagues are not random; they are a systematic dismantling of Egypt's pantheon. YHWH is not merely superior to Egypt's gods; He judges them. In the divine council framework (Deut 32:8-9, Ps 82), the gods of the nations are real spiritual beings under YHWH's authority who have rebelled. The exodus is cosmic warfare.

verses.flow: And the blood shall be a sign for you on the houses where you live; and when I see the blood I will pass over you, and no plague will come upon you to destroy you when I strike the land of Egypt.
verses.note: "When I see the blood, I will pass over you" ('ufasachti aleikhem'). The verb 'pasach' does not primarily mean "to skip" or "pass by" -- recent scholarship suggests it means "to stand guard over, to protect" (from an Akkadian cognate). God does not merely pass the house; He stations Himself at the blood-marked door as a guardian, preventing the destroyer ('mashchit', v.23) from entering. The blood does not buy God off -- it signals the household's faith, and God Himself stands as their shield.

verses.flow: For the LORD will pass through to smite the Egyptians; and when He sees the blood on the lintel and on the two doorposts, the LORD will pass over the door and will not allow the destroyer to come in to your houses to smite you.
verses.note: The "destroyer" ('mashchit') is distinguished from YHWH: "The LORD will pass through ... the LORD will pass over the door and not allow the destroyer to come in." This indicates a divine council agent executing God's judgment decree -- a spiritual being under God's authority, distinct from God Himself. Compare 2 Samuel 24:16, 2 Kings 19:35, and 1 Chronicles 21:15, where "the angel of the LORD" functions as the destroyer.

verses.flow: And they baked the dough which they had brought out of Egypt into cakes of unleavened bread. For it had not become leavened, since they were driven out of Egypt and could not delay, nor had they prepared any provisions for themselves.
Now the time that the sons of Israel lived in Egypt was four hundred and thirty years.
verses.note: Israel's sojourn was "430 years" -- Paul references this exact figure in Galatians 3:17 to argue that the law (given at Sinai) cannot annul the promise (given to Abraham). The LXX and Samaritan Pentateuch read "430 years in Egypt and Canaan," splitting the time between the patriarchal period and the Egyptian sojourn. Either way, the precision of the number underscores that God operates on His own timetable, and not one day is wasted.

John 19:33-36 explicitly cites this command as fulfilled in Christ: the soldiers broke the legs of the two criminals, "but when they came to Jesus and saw that He was already dead, they did not break His legs ... that the Scripture might be fulfilled, 'Not a bone of Him shall be broken.'" The Passover lamb must be whole, unbroken, complete -- because the sacrifice it foreshadows is perfect and sufficient. See also Psalm 34:20.

verses.note: "Consecrate to Me every firstborn" ('qaddesh li khol bekhor'). Because God spared Israel's firstborn, every firstborn now belongs to Him. This is the principle of redemption: what God saves, God owns. The firstborn must be "redeemed" ('padah') by a substitute (13:13, 34:20) -- foreshadowing the ultimate redemption of God's firstborn people through the Firstborn Son (Col 1:15, Heb 1:6). Jesus, as Mary's firstborn, was presented at the Temple under this very law (Luke 2:22-23).

verses.flow: Now when Pharaoh had let the people go, God did not lead them by the way of the land of the Philistines, even though it was near; for God said, "The people might change their minds when they see war and return to Egypt."
verses.note: God did not lead them by the 'way of the land of the Philistines' (the direct coastal route, the 'Via Maris') "though it was near." The reason: "lest the people change their minds when they see war and return to Egypt." God knew the Egyptian frontier fortresses (the 'Ways of Horus' fortress chain is archaeologically confirmed) would provoke a battle Israel was not ready for. God sometimes leads by the longer route because the shorter route would destroy us. The "wilderness way" ('derekh hammidbar') is harder but safer.

verses.flow: And Moses took the bones of Joseph with him, for he had made the sons of Israel solemnly swear, saying, "God will surely visit you, and you shall carry my bones from here with you."
verses.note: Moses takes Joseph's bones as promised (Gen 50:25). Joseph had made Israel swear an oath: "God will surely visit you, and you shall carry up my bones from here." This promise, made 400 years earlier, is honored. Joseph died in faith that the exodus would happen (Heb 11:22). His bones are finally buried in Shechem after the conquest (Josh 24:32) -- a testament to patience spanning centuries.

verses.flow: And the LORD was going before them in a pillar of cloud by day to lead them on the way, and in a pillar of fire by night to give them light, that they might travel by day and by night.
verses.note: The pillar of cloud by day and fire by night ('ammud anan ... ammud esh') is one of the most powerful images of divine presence in Scripture. 'Ammud' means "pillar" or "column" -- a visible, tangible manifestation of God leading His people. The cloud provided shade and direction; the fire provided light and warmth. God does not merely point the way -- He goes before them. This is the visible form of the 'Shekinah' (divine dwelling presence), which later fills the tabernacle (40:34-38) and the Temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11). In the NT, the Spirit fulfills this role: guiding, illuminating, and dwelling with God's people (John 14:16-17, Acts 1:8).

verses.flow: He did not take away the pillar of cloud by day, nor the pillar of fire by night, from before the people.
verses.note: "He did not take away the pillar of cloud by day nor the pillar of fire by night from before the people." God's presence is continuous -- day and night, without interruption. This is the OT expression of Jesus' promise: "I am with you always, to the end of the age" (Matt 28:20), and the assurance of Romans 8:38-39 that nothing can separate us from God's love.

verses.note: God commands Israel to "turn back" and camp by the sea -- deliberately creating a trap. He tells Moses that Pharaoh will think "they are wandering aimlessly, the wilderness has shut them in" (v.3). God engineers the geography to provoke Pharaoh's pursuit. The trap is not for Israel but for Egypt -- God is setting the stage for the definitive act of deliverance that will be remembered for all time.

verses.flow: Thus I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and he will chase after them; and I will be honored through Pharaoh and all his army, and the Egyptians will know that I am the LORD." And they did so.
verses.note: "I will be honored through Pharaoh" ('ve'ikkavdah bePar'oh'). The verb 'kavod' (to glorify/be honored) comes from the same root as 'kaved' (heavy) -- the word used for Pharaoh's hardened heart. The wordplay is intentional: Pharaoh made his heart heavy (stubborn); God will make Himself heavy (glorious) through Pharaoh. Pharaoh's resistance becomes the stage for God's glory.

verses.flow: As Pharaoh drew near, the sons of Israel looked, and behold, the Egyptians were marching after them, and they became very frightened; so the sons of Israel cried out to the LORD.
verses.note: Israel sees the Egyptians marching after them and cries out "to the LORD" ('el YHWH') -- prayer in crisis. But they also turn on Moses with bitter sarcasm: "Is it because there were no graves in Egypt that you have taken us to die in the wilderness?" The irony is layered: Egypt, land of the pyramids and elaborate tombs, had no shortage of graves. Fear produces both prayer and complaint simultaneously -- a thoroughly human response.

verses.flow: But Moses said to the people, "Do not fear! Stand firm and see the salvation of the LORD which He will accomplish for you today; for the Egyptians whom you have seen today, you will never see them again forever.
verses.note: "Do not fear! Stand firm and see the salvation of the LORD which He will accomplish for you today" ('hityatsvu ur'u et yeshu'at YHWH'). The word 'yeshu'ah' (salvation/deliverance) is the root of the name 'Yeshua' -- Jesus. Every time the OT speaks of God's salvation, it speaks the name of Christ. The command is to stand still and watch God work -- a radical reversal of human instinct. Deliverance is God's action, not ours. See also 2 Chronicles 20:17, where Jehoshaphat receives the same instruction.

verses.flow: The LORD will fight for you while you keep silent."
verses.note: "The LORD will fight for you, and you have only to be silent" ('YHWH yillachem lakhem ve'attem tacharishun'). This is the foundational statement of holy war theology in the OT: God is the warrior, Israel is the beneficiary. The Hebrew 'tacharishun' (be silent/still) implies ceasing from their own efforts. The Red Sea crossing is the paradigmatic "God fights, you watch" event. The NT transposes this into spiritual warfare: "the battle is the LORD's" (1 Sam 17:47), and believers "stand firm" in God's armor (Eph 6:13).

verses.flow: And the angel of God, who had been going before the camp of Israel, moved and went behind them; and the pillar of cloud moved from before them and stood behind them.
verses.note: The angel of God and the pillar of cloud move from in front of Israel to behind them -- placing themselves between Israel and the Egyptian army. God's presence becomes a shield. The cloud brings darkness to Egypt but light to Israel (v.20). The same presence that guides also protects, and the same reality that saves Israel judges Egypt. The cross functions the same way: salvation for those who believe, judgment for those who reject (2 Cor 2:15-16).

verses.flow: Then Moses stretched out his hand over the sea; and the LORD swept the sea back by a strong east wind all night and turned the sea into dry land, so the waters were divided.
verses.note: The LORD drove back the sea "by a strong east wind all night" ('beruach qadim azzah kol hallailah'). God uses natural means (wind) for supernatural purposes. The combination of natural agency and divine sovereignty is characteristic of biblical miracles -- God is Lord of nature, not opposed to it. The 'ruach' (wind/spirit) over the waters recalls Genesis 1:2, where the 'Ruach Elohim' (Spirit of God) hovers over the deep. Creation and redemption are parallel acts of divine power over chaos.

verses.flow: And the sons of Israel went through the midst of the sea on the dry ground, and the waters were like a wall to them on their right hand and on their left.
verses.note: Israel walks through the sea on dry ground with "a wall of water on their right and on their left." Paul interprets this explicitly as a type of baptism: "Our fathers were all under the cloud and all passed through the sea, and all were baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea" (1 Cor 10:1-2). The structure is striking: passing through water = death to the old life (Egypt/slavery/sin), emerging on the other side = resurrection to new life (freedom/covenant/ promised land). Christian baptism follows the same pattern: burial with Christ in water, rising to walk in newness of life (Rom 6:3-4).

verses.flow: And when Israel saw the great power which the LORD had used against the Egyptians, the people feared the LORD, and they believed in the LORD and in His servant Moses.
verses.note: "Israel saw the great work ('hayyad haggedolah' -- literally "the great hand") which the LORD had done against the Egyptians, and the people feared the LORD, and they believed in the LORD and in His servant Moses." This is the climax of the exodus narrative: the people respond with fear (reverence), faith, and acknowledgment of God's chosen mediator. The "great hand" of God -- referenced throughout Deuteronomy (4:34, 5:15, 7:19, 26:8) -- becomes Israel's defining memory. Yet this faith proves tragically fragile: within three days they complain at Marah (15:23-24), and within weeks they build the golden calf (ch. 32). Seeing miracles does not guarantee enduring faith -- a warning Jesus Himself echoes in John 6:26-36.

verses.flow: Then Moses and the sons of Israel sang this song to the LORD, and they said: "I will sing to the LORD, for He is highly exalted; the horse and its rider He has hurled into the sea.
verses.note: SONG OF MOSES: The first extended song in Scripture, celebrating YHWH as divine warrior. The Hebrew shirah (song) is in the imperfect tense -- "I will sing" -- making this both memorial and prophetic. The song structure moves from past deliverance (vv. 1-12) to future hope (vv. 13-18). CHRIST: Revelation 15:3 -- the redeemed sing "the song of Moses, the bond-servant of God, and the song of the Lamb," uniting Exodus deliverance with final redemption.

verses.flow: The LORD is my strength and my song, and He has become my salvation; this is my God, and I will praise Him, my father's God, and I will exalt Him.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Yah" (shortened form of YHWH) appears here for the first time. "He has become my salvation" (hayah li lishuah) -- the root "yeshuah" (salvation) is the basis of the name Yeshua/Jesus. The song declares that God Himself -- not merely His acts -- IS salvation. CHRIST: The name Jesus (Yeshua) means "YHWH saves" (Matt 1:21).

verses.flow: Who is like You among the gods, O LORD? Who is like You, majestic in holiness, awesome in praises, working wonders?
verses.note: INCOMPARABILITY: "Who is like You among the gods (elim)?" -- not a denial that other divine beings exist (the Hebrew acknowledges their existence as members of the divine council), but an assertion that YHWH is utterly without peer. "Majestic in holiness" (ne'edar baqqodesh) -- holiness is God's defining attribute. Cf. Ps 89:6-8; Isa 40:25.

verses.flow: In Your lovingkindness You have led the people whom You have redeemed; in Your strength You have guided them to Your holy habitation.
verses.note: LOVINGKINDNESS: Hebrew "chesed" -- covenant loyalty, faithful love. This is the first occurrence of chesed applied to God's redemptive action. The word will become central to Israel's theology (Ps 136 uses it 26 times). "Holy habitation" (neveh qodshekha) points forward to the tabernacle (ch. 25-40) and ultimately to heaven itself.

verses.flow: You will bring them and plant them in the mountain of Your inheritance, the place, O LORD, which You have made for Your dwelling, the sanctuary, O Lord, which Your hands have established.
verses.note: PROPHETIC: This verse anticipates the temple ("the mountain of Your inheritance," "the sanctuary which Your hands have established"). The language echoes Canaanite descriptions of the divine mountain (cf. Baal's Mount Zaphon), but here YHWH claims the true cosmic mountain. CHRIST: Hebrews 12:22 -- believers have come to "Mount Zion, the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem."

verses.flow: The LORD shall reign forever and ever."
verses.note: ETERNAL REIGN: "The LORD shall reign forever and ever" (YHWH yimlok le'olam va'ed) -- the climax of the song. This declaration becomes the foundation of Israel's kingship theology and is echoed in the final Hallel psalms (Ps 146:10). CHRIST: Rev 11:15 -- "The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ, and He will reign forever and ever."

verses.note: THE TREE: Moses is shown a tree (ets) which makes bitter water sweet. The rabbis saw this as Torah (instruction) transforming bitterness. The early church saw the cross -- the "tree" (ets, Acts 5:30; 1 Pet 2:24) that transforms the bitterness of sin into the sweetness of salvation. "I am the LORD who heals you" (YHWH rophe'ekha) -- one of the compound names of God.

verses.flow: Then the LORD said to Moses, "Behold, I will rain bread from heaven for you; and the people shall go out and gather a day's portion every day, that I may test them, whether they will walk in My instruction or not.
verses.note: BREAD FROM HEAVEN: "Lechem min-hashamayim" (bread from heaven) -- the manna is God's direct provision, bypassing natural agriculture. The daily gathering tests obedience and trust. CHRIST: Jesus explicitly identifies Himself as the true bread from heaven: "I am the bread of life... I am the living bread that came down out of heaven" (John 6:35, 51). The manna was a type; Christ is the reality.

verses.note: FORTY YEARS: Israel ate manna for forty years -- the entire wilderness period. The daily miracle sustained an entire nation. CHRIST: Jesus sustains His people daily with His presence: "I am with you always, even to the end of the age" (Matt 28:20).

verses.flow: Behold, I will stand before you there on the rock at Horeb; and you shall strike the rock, and water will come out of it, that the people may drink." And Moses did so in the sight of the elders of Israel.
verses.note: THE ROCK STRUCK: God stands on the rock at Horeb, and Moses strikes it -- the water flows from the very place where God stood. Paul makes the typology explicit: "They drank from a spiritual rock which followed them; and the rock was Christ" (1 Cor 10:4). The rock is struck ONCE (here), and in Numbers 20 Moses is told to SPEAK to the rock but strikes it instead -- a violation of the type, since Christ was struck (crucified) once for all (Heb 9:28; 10:10). Moses' punishment (not entering the land) underscores the seriousness of distorting the typology.

verses.note: MASSAH AND MERIBAH: "Testing" (Massah) and "Quarrel" (Meribah) -- these names become a permanent warning: "Do not harden your hearts as at Meribah, as in the day of Massah in the wilderness" (Ps 95:8). Hebrews 3:7-11 applies this to believers: "Today, if you hear His voice, do not harden your hearts."

verses.note: EAGLES' WINGS: "I bore you on eagles' wings" (al-kanfei nesharim) -- the image of God as a great eagle carrying His young. Deuteronomy 32:11 expands this: the eagle stirs up its nest, hovers over its young, spreads its wings, catches them, bears them on its pinions. God's deliverance is both powerful and tender.

verses.flow: So it came about on the third day, when it was morning, that there were thunder and lightning flashes and a thick cloud upon the mountain and a very loud trumpet sound, so that all the people who were in the camp trembled.
verses.note: THEOPHANY: Thunder (qolot), lightning (beraqim), thick cloud (anan kaved), trumpet blast (qol shofar) -- the fullest theophanic display in the Old Testament. Every sense is overwhelmed. The mountain shakes. The purpose: to impress upon Israel the absolute holiness and unapproachable majesty of God. CHRIST: Hebrews 12:18-24 contrasts Sinai (terror, distance) with Zion (grace, access through Jesus' blood).

verses.flow: "I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.
verses.note: PREAMBLE: The Ten Commandments begin not with law but with grace: "I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt." Obedience is a RESPONSE to deliverance, not a prerequisite for it. God saved Israel first, then gave the law. This is the gospel pattern: salvation by grace precedes sanctification through obedience (Eph 2:8-10).

verses.flow: You shall have no other gods before Me.
verses.note: FIRST COMMANDMENT: "No other gods before My face" (al-panay) -- literally "before My face," meaning in My presence. Since God is omnipresent, this is absolute. The Hebrew does not say "there are no other gods" but "you shall have no other gods." The divine council includes other elohim (Ps 82:1; 89:6-7), but Israel must worship YHWH alone.

verses.flow: You shall not make for yourself an idol, or any likeness of what is in heaven above or on the earth beneath or in the water under the earth.
verses.note: SECOND COMMANDMENT: The prohibition against images (pesel) is not against art but against representing God in created form. Any image reduces the infinite to the finite. The golden calf (ch. 32) violates this command immediately. CHRIST: "He is the image (eikon) of the invisible God" (Col 1:15) -- only God Himself can image God. The Incarnation is the only authorized "image" of God.

verses.flow: You shall not worship them or serve them; for I, the LORD your God, am a jealous God, visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children, on the third and the fourth generations of those who hate Me,

You shall not take the name of the LORD your God in vain, for the LORD will not leave him unpunished who takes His name in vain.
verses.note: THIRD COMMANDMENT: "Taking God's name in vain" (lashav) means emptying it of meaning -- using it frivolously, falsely, or manipulatively. Swearing falsely by God's name, invoking God for magic or deception, or claiming divine authority for human agendas all violate this command.

verses.flow: Remember the sabbath day, to keep it holy.
verses.note: FOURTH COMMANDMENT: "Remember" (zakhor) implies the sabbath was already known (cf. Gen 2:2-3; Exod 16:23-30). The sabbath is both a creation ordinance and a redemption sign (Deut 5:15 links it to the Exodus). CHRIST: Jesus declared Himself "Lord of the Sabbath" (Mark 2:28). Hebrews 4:9-10 speaks of a "sabbath rest" that remains for God's people -- the rest found in Christ.

verses.flow: Honor your father and your mother, that your days may be prolonged in the land which the LORD your God gives you.
verses.note: FIFTH COMMANDMENT: The only commandment with a promise attached (Eph 6:2-3). Honoring parents (kaved, "to give weight/glory to") bridges the vertical (God) and horizontal (neighbor) tables of the law. The family is the foundation of society.

verses.flow: You shall not murder.
verses.note: SIXTH COMMANDMENT: "Lo tirtsach" -- the verb "ratsach" specifically means unlawful killing (murder), not all killing (which would be "harag" or "hemit"). The law itself prescribes capital punishment (21:12), war (Deut 20), and self-defense. CHRIST: Jesus deepens this to include anger and contempt (Matt 5:21-22) -- murder begins in the heart.

verses.flow: You shall not covet your neighbor's house; you shall not covet your neighbor's wife or his male servant or his female servant or his ox or his donkey or anything that belongs to your neighbor."
verses.note: TENTH COMMANDMENT: "Lo tachmod" (do not covet) -- the only commandment addressing internal desire rather than external action. It reveals that God's law governs the heart, not just behavior. Paul identifies coveting as the sin that exposed his own depravity (Rom 7:7-8). CHRIST: The law's ultimate function is to reveal the impossibility of self-righteousness, driving us to Christ (Gal 3:24).

verses.note: 'LET THEM MAKE ME A SANCTUARY, THAT I MAY DWELL AMONG THEM' — The purpose of the tabernacle in one sentence. God does not need a house; He wants nearness. The Hebrew 'shakan' (dwell) gives us 'Shekinah.' CHRIST: 'The Word became flesh and tabernacled (eskenosen) among us' (John 1:14). God's ultimate dwelling is in human flesh.

verses.flow: According to all that I am going to show you, as the pattern of the tabernacle and the pattern of all its furniture, just so you shall construct it.

And for the other side shall be hangings of fifteen cubits, with their three pillars and their three sockets.

All the pillars around the court shall be banded with silver, their hooks of silver and their sockets of bronze.
The length of the court shall be one hundred cubits, and the width fifty throughout, and the height five cubits of fine twisted linen, and their sockets of bronze.

verses.flow: Then bring near to yourself Aaron your brother, and his sons with him, from among the sons of Israel, to minister as priest to Me -- Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, Eleazar and Ithamar, Aaron's sons.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb haqrev (bring near) is the root of qorban (sacrifice/offering). Aaron is literally "offered" to God for priestly service. The four sons are named -- Nadab and Abihu will later die for offering "strange fire" (Lev 10:1-2), while Eleazar and Ithamar continue the priestly line.

verses.flow: And you shall make holy garments for Aaron your brother, for glory and for beauty.
verses.note: KEY PHRASE: "For glory and for beauty" (lekhavod uletif'aret). These are not decorative afterthoughts but theological statements. The priestly garments reflect God's own glory (kavod) and beauty (tif'eret). CHRIST TYPOLOGY: Christ as high priest is "the radiance of God's glory" (Heb 1:3) -- what Aaron's garments symbolized, Christ embodies.

verses.note: SIX GARMENTS: The ephod, breastpiece, robe, checkered tunic, turban, and sash. Four are unique to the high priest (ephod, breastpiece, robe, turban plate); two are shared with ordinary priests (tunic, sash). The high priest alone mediates the fullness of God's presence.

verses.note: URIM AND THUMMIM: Literally "Lights and Perfections" (or "Lights and Completions"). verses.note: THEOLOGY: "Aaron shall bear the iniquity of the holy things." Even Israel's best offerings carry guilt (avon). verses.flow: For seven days you shall make atonement for the altar and consecrate it; then the altar shall be most holy, and whatever touches the altar shall be holy.
verses.note: MOST HOLY: Qodesh qodashim -- "holy of holies." The altar achieves the highest sanctity grade. "Whatever touches the altar shall be holy" -- holiness is contagious. This principle drives the entire graduated-holiness system of the tabernacle.

It shall be a continual burnt offering throughout your generations at the doorway of the tent of meeting before the LORD, where I will meet with you, to speak to you there.
verses.note: MEETING PLACE: "Where I will meet with you, to speak to you there." The burnt offering altar is not merely a place of sacrifice but of divine encounter. God meets His people at the place of substitutionary death. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: "The Word became flesh and tabernacled among us" (John 1:14) -- God meets humanity at the cross.

verses.flow: And they shall know that I am the LORD their God who brought them out of the land of Egypt, that I might dwell among them; I am the LORD their God.
verses.note: PURPOSE STATEMENT: The exodus was not ultimately about liberation from Egypt but about God dwelling with His people. "That I might dwell among them" -- the purpose clause that governs the entire second half of Exodus. Freedom is not the goal; God's presence is the goal. Freedom is the means.

verses.flow: "When you take a census of the sons of Israel to number them, then each one of them shall give a ransom for his soul to the LORD when you number them, so that there will be no plague among them when you number them.
verses.note: RANSOM: Kopher nephesh -- "ransom for the soul." Every Israelite owes God a life. The half-shekel is a symbolic ransom acknowledging that each person's life belongs to God and must be "bought back." The plague threat (negeph) reveals that numbering the people presumes ownership that belongs to God alone.

verses.flow: This is what everyone who is numbered shall give: half a shekel according to the shekel of the sanctuary -- the shekel is twenty gerahs -- half a shekel as a contribution to the LORD.

verses.flow: The rich shall not give more and the poor shall not give less than the half shekel, when you give the contribution to the LORD to make atonement for your souls.
verses.note: EQUALITY: Rich and poor give the same amount. Before God, every soul has the same value. No one can pay more to receive more access; no one is too poor to be ransomed. This contradicts every human honor system.

verses.flow: Then the LORD said to Moses, "Take for yourself spices -- stacte and onycha and galbanum -- spices with pure frankincense; there shall be an equal part of each.
verses.note: INCENSE RECIPE: Stacte (nataph), onycha (shecheleth), galbanum (chelbenah), and frankincense (levonah) in equal parts. Galbanum has an unpleasant odor by itself but enhances the blend -- a rabbinic lesson that the community includes even difficult members. The incense is "salted" (memullach), connecting it to the salt of the covenant (Lev 2:13).

verses.flow: Now the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,
"See, I have called by name Bezalel the son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah.
verses.note: CALLED BY NAME: "I have called by name Bezalel" -- qara' beshem indicates personal election for a specific purpose. Bezalel means "in the shadow of God." verses.note: SABBATH SIGN: The Sabbath is called an ot (sign) -- the same word used for the rainbow (Gen 9:12) and circumcision (Gen 17:11). It is a covenant marker between God and Israel. verses.flow: Now when the people saw that Moses delayed to come down from the mountain, the people assembled about Aaron and said to him, "Come, make us a god who will go before us; as for this Moses, the man who brought us up from the land of Egypt, we do not know what has become of him."
verses.note: APOSTASY: "Make us a god" (elohim) -- the plural can mean "gods" or "a god." The people do not explicitly reject YHWH but demand a visible representation. "As for this Moses" -- the contemptuous demonstrative (zeh Mosheh) distances them from their leader. They reduce Moses to "this man." Their impatience (boshesh = "delayed/was ashamed") reveals faith that depends on a human mediator rather than on God.

verses.flow: And it came about, as soon as he came near the camp, that he saw the calf and the dancing; and Moses' anger burned, and he threw the tablets from his hands and shattered them at the foot of the mountain.
verses.note: SHATTERING: Moses shatters the tablets at the foot of the mountain. This is not a fit of rage but a judicial act -- the covenant document is destroyed because the covenant has been violated. In ancient treaty practice, destroying the treaty tablet formally declared the covenant null and void.

verses.flow: Then I will take My hand away and you shall see My back, but My face shall not be seen."
verses.note: GOD'S BACK: "You shall see My back" (achoray) -- God's "afterglow," the trailing edge of His glory. What Moses sees is what God has done, His attributes made manifest in history, not the unveiled essence of deity. The full vision of God awaits the eschaton: "We shall see Him as He is" (1 John 3:2).

verses.flow: Now the LORD said to Moses, "Cut out for yourself two stone tablets like the former ones, and I will write on the tablets the words that were on the former tablets which you shattered.
verses.note: NEW TABLETS: Moses must cut the new stones himself (God cut the first set, 32:16). The renewed covenant requires human effort -- the initiative is divine, but the participation is mutual. The words will be the same ("the words that were on the former tablets"), but the delivery is different.

verses.flow: And the LORD descended in the cloud and stood there with him as he called upon the name of the LORD.
verses.note: THEOPHANY: "The LORD descended in the cloud and stood there with him." The verb yityatsev (stood/stationed Himself) implies a deliberate, official positioning. God presents Himself formally. This is not a casual encounter but a covenant ceremony.

verses.flow: Then the LORD passed by before him and proclaimed, "The LORD, the LORD God, compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in lovingkindness and truth;
verses.note: THE DIVINE NAME: This is THE self-revelation of God's character -- the most quoted Old Testament passage within the Old Testament itself (Num 14:18; Neh 9:17; Ps 86:15; 103:8; 145:8; Joel 2:13; Jonah 4:2; Nahum 1:3; etc.). "Compassionate" (rachum, from rechem = womb) -- God's mercy is maternal. "Gracious" (channun) -- God gives undeserved favor. "Slow to anger" (erekh appayim, lit. "long of nostrils") -- God's anger is slow to heat. "Abounding in lovingkindness" (rav chesed) -- steadfast covenant love in overwhelming measure. "And truth" (ve'emet) -- faithful reliability.

verses.flow: who keeps lovingkindness for thousands, who forgives iniquity, transgression, and sin; yet He will by no means leave the guilty unpunished, visiting the iniquity of fathers on the children and on the grandchildren to the third and fourth generations."
verses.note: TENSION: "Forgiving iniquity, transgression, and sin; yet He will by no means leave the guilty unpunished." Three words for sin (avon = twisted guilt, pesha' = willful rebellion, chatta'ah = missing the mark) and complete forgiveness -- yet also complete justice. How can God both forgive and punish? This tension drives the entire biblical narrative toward the cross, where both attributes are fully satisfied in Christ's substitutionary death (Rom 3:25-26).

verses.flow: Then He said, "Behold, I am going to make a covenant. Before all your people I will perform miracles which have not been produced in all the earth nor among any of the nations; and all the people among whom you live will see the working of the LORD, for it is a fearful thing that I am going to perform with you.
verses.note: RENEWED COVENANT: "Miracles which have not been produced in all the earth" -- the conquest of Canaan will surpass even the exodus in displays of divine power. The Hebrew nora' (fearful/awesome) describes events that produce awe and terror simultaneously.

verses.flow: But rather, you are to tear down their altars and smash their sacred pillars and cut down their Asherim
-- for you shall not worship any other god, for the LORD, whose name is Jealous, is a jealous God --
verses.note: JEALOUS GOD: "The LORD, whose name is Jealous" (YHWH Qanna' shemo). God's name IS Jealous -- this is not an attribute but an identity marker. Divine jealousy is not petty insecurity but fierce covenant love that will not share its beloved with rivals. The same passion that drives a husband to guard his marriage drives God to guard His covenant.

verses.note: FORTY DAYS: Moses' second forty-day fast on Sinai. He "did not eat bread or drink water" -- sustained entirely by God's presence. The "Ten Commandments" (aseret haddevarim, lit. "ten words") -- the term Decalogue comes from the Greek translation of this Hebrew phrase.

verses.flow: And it came about when Moses was coming down from Mount Sinai -- and the two tablets of the testimony were in Moses' hand as he was coming down from the mountain -- that Moses did not know that the skin of his face shone because of his speaking with Him.
verses.note: SHINING FACE: Qaran -- "to send out rays/horns." The Vulgate translated this as cornuta (horned), leading to centuries of artistic depictions of Moses with horns (including Michelangelo's famous statue). The correct meaning is that Moses' face emitted light -- he was irradiated by proximity to divine glory. Paul interprets this in 2 Corinthians 3:7-18 as a fading glory that contrasts with the permanent glory of the new covenant in Christ.

verses.flow: When Moses had finished speaking with them, he put a veil over his face.
verses.note: THE VEIL: Moses veils his face after speaking God's words to the people, then removes it when speaking with God. Paul's interpretation (2 Cor 3:13-16) is that the veil hid the fading of the glory, and that this veil remains over the hearts of those who read Moses without seeing Christ. "When one turns to the Lord, the veil is taken away" (2 Cor 3:16).

verses.flow: Then Moses assembled all the congregation of the sons of Israel, and said to them, "These are the things which the LORD has commanded you to do:
For six days work may be done, but on the seventh day you shall have a holy day, a sabbath of complete rest to the LORD; whoever does any work on it shall be put to death.
verses.note: SABBATH FIRST: The construction instructions are preceded by the Sabbath command. Even the most sacred building project in history must stop for Sabbath. Work for God does not override rest in God. The death penalty (v. 2) for violation underscores that Sabbath is not a suggestion.

verses.flow: You shall not kindle a fire in any of your dwellings on the sabbath day."

verses.flow: 'Take from among you a contribution to the LORD; whoever is of a willing heart, let him bring it as the LORD's contribution: gold, silver, and bronze,
verses.note: WILLING HEART: Nediv lev -- "generous/noble of heart." The tabernacle is funded entirely by voluntary gifts. God commands the project but does not tax the people for it. The offering comes from the overflow of hearts moved by God's Spirit. Compare 2 Corinthians 9:7: "God loves a cheerful giver."

It requires the mercy seat above it to be whole. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: The ark contained the law (tablets), provision (manna, Heb 9:4), and authority (Aaron's rod) -- Christ fulfills all three.

God watches over His word to perform it. The seven lamps likely represent completeness and divine illumination (cf. Rev 4:5).

and a bulb under the first pair of branches coming out of it, and a bulb under the second pair of branches coming out of it, and a bulb under the third pair of branches coming out of it, for the six branches coming out of the lampstand.

verses.note: WOMEN'S MIRRORS: The laver was made from "the mirrors of the serving women who served at the doorway of the tent of meeting." These women (tsov'ot) are mentioned nowhere else except 1 Samuel 2:22. They gave up instruments of vanity (bronze mirrors) to create an instrument of purification. The transformation of self-reflection into priestly cleansing is deeply symbolic.

verses.flow: All the gold that was used for the work, in all the work of the sanctuary, even the gold of the wave offering, was twenty-nine talents and seven hundred and thirty shekels, according to the shekel of the sanctuary.
verses.note: GOLD: 29 talents and 730 shekels -- approximately 2,200 pounds (1,000 kg) of gold. At modern prices, this represents an extraordinary wealth for a recently liberated slave population, confirming the "plundering of Egypt" (Exod 12:35-36).

verses.note: SILVER: 100 talents and 1,775 shekels from the census ransom (v. 26). The 100 talents became the 100 sockets -- the foundation of the tabernacle literally rests on atonement money. The structure stands on ransom.

verses.flow: a beka a head -- that is, half a shekel according to the shekel of the sanctuary -- for each one who passed over to those who were numbered, from twenty years old and upward, for six hundred and three thousand, five hundred and fifty men.
verses.note: CENSUS COUNT: 603,550 men twenty and older -- matching the number in Numbers 1:46. At a half-shekel each, this produces exactly 301,775 shekels (100 talents + 1,775 shekels), confirming the arithmetic.

verses.note: OBEDIENCE REFRAIN: The phrase "just as the LORD had commanded Moses" appears seven times in this chapter (vv. 1, 5, 7, 21, 26, 29, 31) and carries through into chapter 40. Nezer is the same word used for the Nazirite's consecrated hair (Num 6:7) and for the royal crown (2 Sam 1:10; 2 Kings 11:12). The high priest wears both priestly holiness and royal authority.

verses.flow: Thus all the work of the tabernacle of the tent of meeting was completed; and the sons of Israel did according to all that the LORD had commanded Moses; so they did.
verses.note: COMPLETION FORMULA: "Thus all the work...was completed" (vattekhal). The verb kalah (to finish/complete) echoes Genesis 2:1-2: "Thus the heavens and the earth were completed." The tabernacle construction is a new creation -- a micro-cosmos where heaven and earth meet.

verses.flow: And Moses examined all the work, and behold, they had done it; just as the LORD had commanded, so they had done. And Moses blessed them.
verses.note: MOSES' BLESSING: "And Moses blessed them" parallels God's blessing of the creation work in Genesis 1:28 and 2:3. Moses functions here as God's representative, pronouncing divine approval on the completed work. The tabernacle is Eden rebuilt -- a place where God and humanity dwell together.

verses.flow: Then the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,
"On the first day of the first month you shall set up the tabernacle of the tent of meeting.
verses.note: FIRST DAY: The tabernacle is erected on "the first day of the first month" -- New Year's Day, exactly one year after the exodus (cf. Exod 12:2). A new era begins. The first year brought liberation; the second brings habitation. God's dwelling with His people marks a new creation.

verses.note: THE TESTIMONY: Moses places "the testimony" (ha'edut = the tablets of the law) into the ark. The law rests inside the mercy seat -- justice is enclosed within mercy. The structure itself makes a theological statement: God's judgment is real but contained within His grace.

verses.note: SCREENING THE ARK: Moses "screened off the ark of the testimony" with the veil. The most holy object is hidden from view. God dwells among His people but remains inaccessible to casual approach. The veil will only be torn at the cross (Matt 27:51).

verses.flow: And he erected the court all around the tabernacle and the altar, and hung up the veil for the gateway of the court. Thus Moses finished the work.
verses.note: MOSES FINISHED: Vayekhal Mosheh -- "Moses finished the work." The exact phrase used for God finishing creation (Gen 2:2, vayekhal Elohim). Moses completes the earthly copy of the heavenly pattern shown on Sinai (25:9, 40). The builder finishes what the Architect designed.

words.g: to fill or be full of

verses.flow: Then the cloud covered the tent of meeting, and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle.
verses.note: THE GLORY FILLS: "The cloud covered the tent of meeting, and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle." This is the climax of the entire book of Exodus. The kavod (glory) that appeared on Sinai (24:16-17) now descends to dwell among the people. FULFILLMENT: Solomon's temple (1 Kings 8:10-11), the incarnation (John 1:14 -- "The Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, and we beheld His glory"), and the eschatological temple (Rev 21:3 -- "Behold, the tabernacle of God is among men").

words.g: to fill or be full of

verses.flow: And Moses was not able to enter the tent of meeting because the cloud had settled on it, and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle.
verses.note: OVERWHELMING PRESENCE: Even Moses -- who spoke with God face to face (33:11) -- "was not able to enter." The concentrated presence of God's glory exceeds what the greatest prophet can bear. The same will happen at Solomon's temple dedication (1 Kings 8:11). When God fully inhabits a space, even His servants must withdraw.

verses.flow: And throughout all their journeys whenever the cloud was taken up from over the tabernacle, the sons of Israel would set out;
verses.note: CLOUD GUIDANCE: The cloud becomes Israel's guide for all their journeys. The visible presence of God determines movement and rest. Israel does not navigate by human strategy but by divine presence. When the cloud moves, they move; when it stays, they stay.

verses.flow: but if the cloud was not taken up, then they did not set out until the day when it was taken up.
For the cloud of the LORD was on the tabernacle by day, and there was fire in it by night, in the sight of all the house of Israel, throughout all their journeys.
verses.note: FINAL VERSE: The book that began in slavery ends in glory. Exodus opens with Israel groaning under Pharaoh's whip (1:11-14) and closes with the fire and cloud of God's presence resting over them "throughout all their journeys." The God who heard their cry (2:23-24) now visibly dwells among them. "Cloud by day...fire by night" -- God's presence is continuous, not intermittent. He is with His people in brightness and in darkness, in travel and in rest.
